title: URL Shortener — System Design
description: A step-by-step walkthrough of designing a URL shortener, starting from requirements and capacity, then evolving the architecture from a minimal client/server toward a sharded, edge-accelerated, multi-region system.

patterns.name: Cache-aside
patterns.what: Read from cache first; on a miss, read the source of truth and backfill the cache.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy workloads with a hot subset of keys, where a slightly stale read is acceptable.
patterns.group: Add a Cache for Hot URLs

patterns.name: Sharding (horizontal partitioning)
patterns.what: Split data across nodes by a partition key so no single node holds the whole dataset.
patterns.whenToUse: When data or write volume exceeds what one node can hold or serve.

patterns.name: Edge caching (CDN)
patterns.what: Serve cacheable responses from points of presence close to users.
patterns.whenToUse: Geographically distributed read traffic for cacheable content.

tradeoff: Self-hosted SQL gives transactional alias inserts and familiar tooling but you own sharding, failover, and backups.
Managed partitioned stores handle sharding/replication/elastic throughput but cost rises at 100k+ reads/sec and relational owner queries need secondary indexes.
makesIrrelevant: A managed auto-sharding store can remove the hand-rolled shard router and resharding playbook.

tradeoff: Redis adds data structures (counters, sorted sets) and optional persistence; Memcached is simpler and multithreaded but key/value only with no persistence.
Managed cache removes failover/patching toil at a per-node premium.
Edge functions (Workers/Lambda@Edge) can resolve hot codes without an origin round-trip; self-hosted reverse proxies are cheaper at small scale but you operate the PoPs.
Aggressive edge/browser caching lowers cost but makes click analytics approximate.
Range allocation survives short ID-service outages from a local block but wastes IDs on crash; Snowflake needs clock discipline and machine-id assignment.
Usually self-hosted — it is a few hundred lines, and a managed dependency rarely pays off.
Kafka gives high-throughput replayable logs you operate; managed streams trade per-GB cost for no broker ops.
Either way the redirect path is fire-and-forget, so a queue outage degrades analytics, not redirects.
Columnar OLAP stores answer dimensional/historical queries cheaply; near-real-time totals still live in Redis counters.
Raw retention is short (7–30 days), then only minute/hour/day rollups, so the store sizing follows rollups, not raw events.
Custom aliases are the one namespace needing global uniqueness; a strongly-consistent service reserves them before commit at the cost of cross-region write latency.
Generated codes avoid this entirely via region-scoped ID space, so the reservation service only sits on the vanity-alias path.
makesIrrelevant: If you drop custom aliases (or pin each to a home region), the global reservation service is unnecessary.

tradeoff: Create-time blocklist checks are cheap and synchronous; deep malware/phishing scans run async and update abuse_status later.
Managed reputation APIs cover most known-bad URLs; self-hosted heuristics catch novel campaigns but need tuning.
Track redirect p99, CDN/cache hit ratio, blocked-redirect rate, replication lag, and queue lag as user-visible signals.
Managed monitoring removes scrape/storage ops; self-hosted Prometheus is cheaper and more tunable at scale.

say: Restate the problem: a service that maps long URLs to short codes and redirects.
List functional needs (create, redirect, optional analytics) and the read:write ratio.
Name the non-functional priorities: low-latency redirects, high availability, durability.
Do a back-of-envelope: writes/sec, reads/sec, storage growth over 5 years.
Sketch the API: POST /shorten, GET /{code}. Note redirects are the hot path.
Draw client → LB → app → cache → DB, then justify each addition against the numbers.
Go deep where prompted: ID generation, cache strategy, sharding the mapping store.
Summarize how the design meets each requirement.
Call out the top bottleneck and one thing you'd do next with more time.

expectations: Produces a working single-region design: app servers, a database, a cache.
Can explain why a cache helps a read-heavy workload.
Drives the capacity math and lets it justify each component.
Reasons about ID-generation collisions and sharding the mapping store.
Discusses cache invalidation, TTLs, and stale-read tradeoffs.
Frames the design around multi-region availability and failure domains.
Weighs consistency vs. latency for cross-region writes and analytics.
Quantifies tradeoffs and names the operational cost of each choice.

capacityDiagram: DB[(Mapping Store\n~75 TB raw / 200+ TB replicated)]

functional: Shorten a long URL to a unique short code (e.g. sho.rt/aZ8q1).
Redirect a short code back to its original long URL with low latency.
Optional custom alias (vanity URL) chosen by the user.
Optional expiration time for a short URL.
Click analytics: total clicks, recent clicks, basic referrer/geo breakdown.
Safety: refuse to shorten or redirect malicious targets (phishing, malware, open-redirect abuse); support takedown of blocked links and quarantine of under-review links.
nonFunctional: High availability for redirects (target ≥ 99.99%); creates can be slightly less available.
Low p99 redirect latency (target ≤ 50 ms at the edge for hot links; ≤ 150 ms on origin miss).
Scalable to billions of stored URLs and tens of thousands of redirects/sec.
Durable storage — a shortened URL must not be lost.
Analytics are eventually consistent and approximate when redirects are cached at the edge or browser; the mapping write must be strongly consistent.

capacity.label: Storage (5 years)
capacity.note: ~150B URLs x ~500 bytes/row; plan for 200+ TB with indexes, replicas, and backups

capacity.label: Cache working set
capacity.note: Holds tens to hundreds of millions of hot mappings — the actively-clicked working set, not a fixed percentage of all stored URLs

capacity.label: Click events
capacity.note: One event per redirect (edge + origin). At ~200 bytes raw that is ~1.7 TB/day; keep raw events ~7-30 days, then roll up to per-code minute/hour/day counters and sample high-cardinality dimensions

capacity.label: Bandwidth (egress)
capacity.note: Mostly 302 redirects and edge responses, very small payloads

api.description: Create a short URL from a long URL (auth required; owner derived from the credential). Optional custom alias, TTL, and redirect policy. The target URL is normalized and validated before a code is minted, and a client-supplied Idempotency-Key (scoped per user) makes retries safe: a repeated key with the same body returns the original result, while the same key with a different body is a 409 conflict. redirectPolicy selects temporary (302, default) vs. permanent (301); permanent links cannot use a short TTL, are a poor fit for editable vanity links, and make analytics approximate because cached browsers may never return to the edge. In multi-region active-active mode the response also returns the link's homeRegion so clients and early cross-region redirects can resolve replication lag.
api.response:
{ "shortUrl": "https://sho.rt/aZ8q1", "code": "aZ8q1", "redirectPolicy": "temporary", "homeRegion": "us-east", "expiresAt"?: "ISO-8601" }
Errors: 400 invalid/unsafe URL, 409 alias taken/reserved or idempotency-key reused with a different body, 422 disallowed scheme or permanent+short-TTL combination, 429 quota exceeded

api.description: Resolve a short code and redirect to the long URL. Uses the link's redirect_policy: 302 by default when click tracking matters; 301 for permanent, less-trackable links. The safety gate runs before redirecting: blocked and under-review links never forward to their target.
api.response:
HTTP 302 (or 301 for permanent links) with Location: <longUrl> for active links.
404 if unknown, expired, or deleted.
blocked: 451 Unavailable For Legal Reasons (or a warning interstitial) with no redirect.
under_review: 200 warning interstitial that requires explicit click-through, never an automatic redirect.

api.description: Fetch metadata for a short link (owner, createdAt, expiry, click counters).
Soft-delete a short link (owner-only). Future redirects return 404.

messages.label: UPDATE urls SET deleted_at=now(), status='deleted' WHERE code=?

api.description: Internal-only safety endpoint (not public). Called by trust-and-safety operators and the automated abuse scanner to block or quarantine a code. It sets status to blocked or under_review with a blocked_reason, deletes the regional cache entry, and enqueues a durable purge_job so the CDN/edge are purged and retried until acknowledged. The redirect safety gate then refuses to forward the link everywhere. Access is restricted to internal networks/service credentials; it is never exposed on the public API.
api.request:
Headers: Authorization (internal service or operator credential)
Body: { "code": "aZ8q1", "action": "block" | "review", "reason": "phishing" | "malware" | "manual_takedown" }
api.response:
{ "code": "aZ8q1", "status": "blocked", "purgeId": "<uuid>" }
Errors: 401/403 if not an authorized internal caller, 404 unknown code

dataModel.name: urls
dataModel.note: Primary mapping table. Sharded by short_code hash at scale.

fields.name: short_code
fields.note: Base62; primary lookup key

fields.name: user_id
fields.note: Owner; NULL for anonymous

fields.name: status
fields.note: active | expired | deleted | blocked | under_review

fields.name: redirect_policy
fields.note: 302 (default) | 301 permanent; controls cacheability and click observability

fields.name: long_url_hash
fields.note: Hash of the normalized URL; supports dedup/idempotency lookups

fields.name: abuse_status
fields.note: clean | suspect | malicious; drives the redirect safety gate

fields.name: last_scanned_at
fields.note: Last malware/phishing scan time

fields.name: blocked_reason
fields.note: Why a link was blocked (phishing, malware, manual takedown)

fields.name: created_ip_hash
fields.note: For per-IP create quotas and abuse correlation

fields.name: home_region
fields.note: Region that owns this mapping's writes; early cross-region misses route here during replication lag

fields.name: code_source
fields.note: generated | alias; generated codes use region-scoped ID space, aliases come from the global reservation namespace

fields.name: deleted_at
fields.note: Soft delete

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Bounded-TTL record of resolved create requests so a retried POST returns the original short code instead of minting a new one. Keyed by (user_id, idempotency_key); request_hash detects a reused key sent with a different body so the API can return a 409 conflict instead of silently returning the wrong result.

fields.name: idempotency_key
fields.note: Client-supplied; part of PK with user_id

fields.name: user_id
fields.note: Part of PK; scopes the key to one user

fields.name: request_hash
fields.note: Hash of the normalized request body; a repeated key with a different hash is an idempotency conflict (409)

fields.name: short_code
fields.note: Result of the original create

fields.name: expires_at
fields.note: Key reusable after this window

dataModel.name: alias_reservations
dataModel.note: Strongly-consistent global namespace for custom (vanity) aliases. A reservation is committed here before any region writes the mapping, so an alias is claimed exactly once across all regions. Generated codes never touch this table.

fields.name: alias
fields.note: The requested vanity code; globally unique

fields.name: user_id
fields.note: Owner that reserved it

fields.name: home_region
fields.note: Region that owns writes for this alias

fields.name: status
fields.note: reserved | active | released

dataModel.name: purge_jobs
dataModel.note: Durable record of CDN/edge cache purges for deletes, expiries, and takedowns. Each row is retried until every edge acknowledges, so a takedown is not silently dropped when a purge call fails.

fields.name: reason
fields.note: delete | expiry | takedown

fields.name: status
fields.note: pending | acked | failed; retried with backoff until acked

dataModel.name: click_counters (rollups)
dataModel.note: Pre-aggregated per-code counters (minute/hour/day) produced by the stream processor. Backs clickCount and dashboards without scanning raw events.

fields.name: short_code
fields.note: Part of PK

fields.name: bucket
fields.note: Rollup window start; part of PK

fields.name: granularity
fields.note: minute | hour | day

dataModel.name: owner_links
dataModel.note: Secondary index for management pages. It avoids querying every short_code shard when an owner lists their links.

dataModel.name: click_events (analytics store)
dataModel.note: Append-only; written via the click queue, not the hot redirect path. Privacy: store hashed/truncated IPs (not raw), keep referrer/user_agent only as long as needed, and honor regional retention/consent rules — raw events age out in ~7-30 days, leaving only aggregate rollups.

fields.name: short_code
fields.note: FK-style; not enforced

functional.requirement: Shorten a long URL to a unique short code.
functional.how: App servers call the ID Generator to mint a base62 code, route the code to the correct mapping shard, then write (short_code -> long_url) with conditional uniqueness. The cache is populated on write so the first redirect is a hit.

functional.requirement: Redirect a short code back to the original URL with low latency.
functional.how: Redirects are served from the CDN edge for hot codes; cold codes fall through to the regional Redis cache, then to one mapping shard. Geo routing sends users to the nearest healthy region.

functional.requirement: Custom alias (vanity URL) chosen by the user.
functional.how: Vanity aliases share the mapping store and go through the same conditional uniqueness check on short_code. In multi-region active-active mode, aliases need home-region routing or a global reservation service.

functional.requirement: Optional expiration for a short URL.
functional.how: expires_at is stored on the row; cache and edge TTLs are set to the lesser of cache policy and remaining lifetime, delete/expiry publishes purge events, and the app returns 404 for expired codes.

functional.requirement: Click analytics: totals, recent activity, basic breakdowns.
functional.how: Edge functions and origin app servers emit click events to the message queue when they observe redirects. A stream processor aggregates per-code counters into Redis and writes events to the analytics store. If a link uses permanent browser-cached 301 redirects, analytics are explicitly approximate.

functional.requirement: Safety: block malicious links and support takedowns.
functional.how: Create-time validation normalizes the target, rejects dangerous schemes, and checks a domain blocklist; suspect URLs are queued for async malware/phishing scanning that sets abuse_status. The redirect safety gate refuses to forward blocked (451) or under-review (interstitial) links. An internal admin/scanner endpoint blocks a code, invalidates the cache, and enqueues a durable purge_job retried until every edge acknowledges.

nonFunctional.how: Stateless app servers behind an HA load balancer, multi-AZ Redis with replicas, replicated mapping shards, a CDN that serves hot redirects when origin is degraded, and geo failover across regions.

nonFunctional.requirement: Low p99 redirect latency (≤ 50 ms at edge; ≤ 150 ms on origin miss).
nonFunctional.how: Edge resolution at the CDN for hot codes; regional Redis cache for the long tail; single-shard primary-key lookup as last resort. Redirects are usually GET -> 302 so expiration/delete and analytics remain practical.

Reads are absorbed by the CDN and Redis hot set; misses route to a single shard. Writes go through a dedicated ID generator and shard router, decoupling write throughput from one DB sequence or one database node.

nonFunctional.requirement: Scale to billions of URLs and tens of thousands of redirects/sec.
nonFunctional.how: App tier scales horizontally behind the load balancer. Cache scales via Redis Cluster sharding. The mapping store shards by short_code hash. ID generation avoids a central DB sequence, and multi-region routing distributes read load globally.

nonFunctional.requirement: Durability — a shortened URL must not be lost.
nonFunctional.how: The mapping is committed to the owning shard before the API returns success. Each shard has replicas, backups, and PITR. Multi-region replication extends durability and availability, while cache and edge entries are treated as rebuildable.

nonFunctional.requirement: Eventually consistent analytics; strong consistency for the mapping write.
nonFunctional.how: Mapping writes are synchronous to the owning shard in the write region; global visibility can be eventual across regions. Analytics are produced from an async event stream and the hot read path never blocks on the analytics pipeline.

followUps: How would you support custom (vanity) aliases without collisions?
How would you prevent abuse (phishing, malware-hosting URLs)?
How would the system behave during a database failover?
When would you choose 301 vs 302 redirects, and what does that do to analytics and expiration?
How would you reshard the mapping store without downtime?
How would you make redirects work across regions with low latency?
How would you make custom aliases globally unique in an active-active multi-region design?
How would you change the design for a 10× growth in QPS?
How would you support per-user analytics dashboards in near real time?
How would you implement rate limiting at the edge?

steps.title: 1. Client and Server
steps.description: Start with the simplest possible architecture: a client (browser or mobile app) talks directly to a single application server. The server handles two operations: creating a short URL from a long URL, and redirecting a short URL back to the long URL.
steps.decisionPrompt: Design the smallest API boundary for create and redirect: what data enters each request, what response proves success, and which concerns stay out of this first step?

concepts.definition: The agreed request and response shape between client and service.
concepts.whyItMatters: The rest of the design must preserve the create and redirect behavior defined here.

concepts.definition: A response such as 301 or 302 that tells the browser to request another URL.
concepts.whyItMatters: Redirect latency is the main user-visible path for a URL shortener.

options.name: In-memory map (prototype)
options.description: A single server keeps the short_code to long_url mapping in a process-local hash map, serving creates and redirects entirely from memory.
pros: Fastest way to explain the product behavior
No database or distributed systems concepts yet
Good first step for clarifying API semantics
cons: All mappings disappear on restart
Cannot run more than one server safely
No durability, ownership, expiration, or analytics history

view.caption: The client calls the app server, which keeps the entire code-to-URL mapping in an in-memory map with no external store.

options.name: Embedded local store
options.description: A single server persists the mapping to an embedded on-disk store local to the process, so data survives a restart without operating a separate DB tier.
pros: Survives process restart during a prototype demo
Introduces durable writes without operating a separate DB tier
Keeps the first architecture visually simple
cons: Still a single machine and single failure domain
Hard to scale horizontally because state is local
Migration to a real database is unavoidable

view.caption: The client calls the app server, which persists the mapping to an embedded local store living on the same machine.

deepDives.title: Short code generation (initial approach)
points: Random 7-char base62 string; reject on collision and retry.
Alternative: hash(longUrl) and take a prefix — but collisions are likely and identical inputs always map to the same code, which may be undesirable.
Defer a 'real' ID strategy until DB write throughput becomes a bottleneck.

bottlenecks.issue: All state lives in process memory; restart loses everything.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Add persistence (next step).

bottlenecks.issue: Single server is a SPOF and caps throughput.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Add a load balancer + multiple stateless app servers (step 3).

talkingPoints: Clarify HTTP 301 vs 302 — 301 is cacheable by browsers and makes click tracking less accurate. 302 keeps redirect traffic observable but costs more origin/edge work.
Mention CAP only when persistence enters — right now there is no replication to reason about.

flows.name: Shorten in memory
flows.note: The first version proves the API shape but stores the mapping only inside one process.

flows.name: Redirect in memory
flows.note: Redirects are simple lookups, but restart or multi-server scaling breaks this design.

view.caption: The client sends POST /shorten and GET /:code requests to a single app server that handles both create and redirect.

steps.title: 2. Add a Database
steps.description: The server cannot keep mappings in memory — they must survive restarts. Introduce a database (e.g. PostgreSQL or MySQL) to persist the (short_code → long_url) mapping. Each shorten request inserts a row; each redirect performs a lookup by primary key.
steps.decisionPrompt: Choose the durable mapping record and write semantics: what must be committed before returning a short URL, and how do duplicate or retried creates behave?

traps.trap: Assuming UNIQUE(short_code) is enough to make creates safe
traps.why: The unique constraint stops two rows sharing a code, but a client or load balancer retrying POST /shorten still mints a brand-new code each time — one user intent, many short links.
traps.instead: Accept an Idempotency-Key (or deterministic request token) and return the original result for a bounded window.

traps.trap: Storing the long URL exactly as received
traps.why: Un-normalized, unvalidated targets allow dangerous schemes (javascript:, data:), open-redirect abuse, and duplicate entries that differ only by trailing slash or case.
traps.instead: Normalize (scheme, host casing, punycode, default ports) and validate against an allowed-scheme/length policy before minting a code.

concepts.definition: A persistent short_code to long_url record that survives process and machine restarts.
concepts.whyItMatters: A generated short URL is useless if the mapping can disappear after success is returned.

concepts.definition: A database rule that prevents two rows from using the same short code.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the final guard against code collisions even if application retries race.

options.name: SQL database (default)
options.description: The (short_code, long_url) mapping is persisted in a relational database: each create inserts a row and each redirect is a primary-key lookup, with a UNIQUE constraint on aliases.
pros: UNIQUE constraint gives a clean custom-alias collision story
Transactions keep mapping creation simple and correct
Operationally familiar for the first durable version
cons: A single primary becomes a write bottleneck later
Manual sharding is needed at very large cardinality
Cross-region active/active writes are harder

view.caption: The app server stores each mapping in a SQL database that enforces a unique constraint on short_code.

options.name: Managed key-value store
options.description: The mapping is stored in a managed key-value store keyed by short_code, with a separate owner index for dashboards and conditional puts to enforce no-overwrite.
pros: Horizontal scale and partitioning are built in
Primary-key lookups match the redirect workload
Conditional put can enforce no-overwrite semantics
cons: Secondary queries and owner dashboards need extra indexes
Transactional alias flows can be less ergonomic
Cost and hot-partition behavior need careful modeling

view.caption: The app server writes mappings to a managed key-value store and queries a separate owner index for per-user lookups.

deepDives.title: SQL vs NoSQL
points: Workload is keyed lookups by short_code — both can serve it.
SQL gives transactions for 'insert if not exists' on custom aliases and simpler operational tooling.
NoSQL (e.g. DynamoDB, Cassandra) scales horizontally out of the box; consider when single-node DB limits are visible.

deepDives.title: Write path
points: Insert (short_code, long_url, created_at, user_id?). UNIQUE on short_code guarantees no overwrite.
On collision (race or random duplicate), retry with a new code.

deepDives.title: Idempotency and URL normalization
points: Accept an Idempotency-Key per create; store (key -> short_code) with a TTL so a retried POST returns the same code instead of a new one.
Normalize the target before hashing/storing: lowercase host, strip default ports, apply punycode, drop tracking-only fragments per policy.
Validate the scheme (allow http/https only), enforce a max length, and reject obviously dangerous targets (javascript:, data:, internal/link-local hosts).
Optionally dedup by long_url_hash per owner so the same target reuses one code.

bottlenecks.issue: Single DB instance caps writes and reads.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Add read replicas; later shard by short_code hash.

bottlenecks.issue: Every redirect is a DB read.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Add a cache (step 4).

talkingPoints: Mention an index strategy: primary key on short_code is enough for the hot path; created_at index helps analytics queries.
Soft delete (deleted_at) vs hard delete — soft delete is safer if links are shared externally.

flows.name: Shorten — minimal write path
flows.note: Server generates a code, inserts it; UNIQUE constraint catches collisions and we retry.

flows.name: Redirect — minimal read path
flows.note: Every redirect is a DB lookup by primary key. Latency = network + index seek.

view.caption: The client's request goes to the app server, which now reads and writes the code-to-URL mapping in a shared database.

steps.title: 3. Scale Out with a Load Balancer
steps.description: A single app server is a single point of failure and a bottleneck. Place a load balancer in front and run multiple stateless app servers behind it. Sessions and state must live in the database (or cache), not in the app process.
steps.decisionPrompt: Decide how to scale stateless create and redirect traffic behind a load balancer while keeping redirects correct during server failure.

concepts.definition: Increase capacity by adding more stateless service instances behind a routing layer.
concepts.whyItMatters: Redirect traffic can grow by adding app servers instead of making one server larger.
concepts.group: Scale Out with a Load Balancer

concepts.definition: A periodic probe used by the load balancer to remove unhealthy servers from rotation.
concepts.whyItMatters: Users should not be routed to a server that cannot resolve or create links.
concepts.group: Scale Out with a Load Balancer

concepts.definition: A per-IP or per-user rate limit applied to link creation, usually at the edge or load balancer.
concepts.whyItMatters: A shortener's create path is a prime abuse target; quotas stop spam campaigns from flooding the ID generator and storage.
concepts.group: Scale Out with a Load Balancer

concepts.definition: A check on the redirect path that refuses to forward blocked or under-review links.
concepts.whyItMatters: Phishing and malware URLs must be stopped at redirect time, not just flagged in a dashboard.
concepts.group: Scale Out with a Load Balancer

options.name: Managed HA load balancer (default)
options.description: A managed load balancer with health checks and TLS termination fronts a pool of stateless app servers, all sharing the database for state.
pros: Health checks and failover are built in
Supports TLS termination, connection reuse, and autoscaling targets
Keeps app servers stateless and interchangeable
cons: Adds one more managed dependency
Regional LB failure still needs a broader failover plan
Misconfigured health checks can drain healthy capacity

view.caption: A managed highly-available load balancer fans client requests out to app servers 1, 2, and 3, which all read and write the database.

options.name: DNS round-robin
options.description: DNS returns the app servers' addresses in rotation so clients spread requests across them, with no dedicated load balancer in the path.
pros: Very simple and cheap
Removes a dedicated LB from the request path
Can work for early low-risk deployments
cons: Slow client-side failover because DNS is cached
No request-level health checks or connection balancing
Harder to do autoscaling and deployments safely

view.caption: DNS round-robin hands the client one of app servers 1, 2, or 3 directly, each of which talks to the shared database.

deepDives.title: Load balancing algorithm
points: Round-robin or least-connections is fine — app servers are stateless.
Avoid IP-hash unless you need stickiness; it complicates capacity changes.

deepDives.title: Create-side admission and abuse controls
points: Apply per-IP and per-user create quotas (token bucket) at the edge/LB before requests reach app servers — a shortener's create path is a common abuse target.
Validate and normalize the target URL and check it against a domain blocklist on create; queue suspect URLs for async malware/phishing scanning and set abuse_status accordingly.
On redirect, enforce the safety gate: a blocked code returns 451 (or a warning interstitial) and an under-review code returns a click-through interstitial, instead of redirecting.
Rate limiting also protects the ID generator and DB from create floods, not just the redirect path.

deepDives.title: Health checks
points: LB needs a cheap /healthz that checks process liveness, not DB connectivity (so DB blips don't drain the fleet).
Deeper /readyz that checks DB can drive deployment readiness gates.

bottlenecks.issue: LB itself becomes the SPOF.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use a managed/HA load balancer (ALB/NLB, GCLB) or two LBs behind DNS/anycast.

bottlenecks.issue: All servers still hit the same DB.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Read replicas for redirects; cache for hot keys (next step).

bottlenecks.issue: Cold-start spikes when adding servers under high QPS.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Pre-warm connection pools; ramp traffic via weighted target groups.

talkingPoints: Stateless app servers are a prerequisite for autoscaling — no sticky sessions, no in-memory caches that diverge.
Mention TLS termination at the LB and HTTP/2 to the backend.

flows.name: Redirect — through the load balancer
flows.note: Any app server can serve any redirect because they are stateless. LB picks one via round-robin / least-connections.

flows.name: Health check and drain
flows.note: The load balancer must stop sending traffic to a bad process without requiring sticky sessions.

view.caption: The client hits a load balancer that spreads requests across app servers 1, 2, and 3, all sharing the same database.

steps.title: 4. Add a Cache for Hot URLs
steps.description: Redirects vastly outnumber creations, and a small fraction of URLs receive most of the traffic. Add an in-memory cache in front of the database. On a redirect, check the cache first; on a miss, fall back to the database and populate the cache.

traps.trap: Caching every lookup, including one-off long-tail codes
traps.why: Cold codes evict hot ones and inflate memory for keys that are never read again.
traps.instead: Cache on read (cache-aside) so only codes that are actually requested populate the cache.

traps.trap: No TTL or invalidation, so deleted/expired links keep redirecting
traps.why: The cache serves stale mappings long after the source of truth changed.
traps.instead: Set a TTL and invalidate on update/delete; accept a bounded staleness window.

traps.trap: Letting an extremely hot code's TTL expire with no stampede protection
traps.why: When a viral code's entry expires, thousands of concurrent misses hit the same shard at once (cache stampede), and synchronized TTLs make whole batches expire together.
traps.instead: Coalesce concurrent misses for the same key (singleflight), jitter TTLs so they do not expire in lockstep, and serve-stale-while-revalidate for the hottest codes.

steps.decisionPrompt: Decide which redirect lookups deserve cache-aside treatment, how misses fall back to the mapping store, and how stale or deleted links are handled.

concepts.definition: Application code checks cache first, reads the database on miss, then backfills the cache.
concepts.whyItMatters: Hot redirects avoid database reads while correctness still falls back to the mapping store.
concepts.group: Add a Cache for Hot URLs

concepts.definition: A time-to-live after which a cached value expires automatically.
concepts.whyItMatters: TTL limits stale redirects and bounds cache memory without requiring perfect invalidation.
concepts.group: Add a Cache for Hot URLs

options.name: Redis (default)
options.description: A Redis cache fronts the database for redirects; a hit returns immediately and a miss falls back to the DB and populates the cache, with optional persistence and Cluster sharding.
pros: Rich data types (sorted sets, hashes) help with counters and rate limiting
Optional persistence (AOF/RDB) avoids cold caches on restart
Built-in replication and Cluster mode for HA and sharding
cons: Single-threaded per shard — large values or slow commands block the loop
Slightly higher operational complexity than Memcached

view.caption: App servers check a Redis cache before the database on a redirect, falling back to the database on a miss.

options.name: Memcached
options.description: A multithreaded Memcached tier caches hot code-to-URL lookups in front of the DB, with clients sharding keys via consistent hashing and no persistence.
pros: Multithreaded — handles high concurrent throughput on a single node
Simple, predictable LRU semantics
Lower memory overhead per key
cons: Key/value only — no data structures, no atomic counters beyond incr
No persistence — restart wipes the cache (thundering herd risk)
No native replication; clients shard via consistent hashing

view.caption: App servers check a Memcached cache before the database on a redirect, falling back to the database on a miss.

deepDives.title: Caching policy
points: Read-through with TTL (e.g. 24h) is the simplest; popular keys auto-refresh.
Write-through on create populates the cache so the first redirect is a hit.
Negative caching (404s) prevents the DB from being hammered by scanners.
For very hot keys, coalesce concurrent misses (singleflight) and jitter TTLs so they don't all expire together; serve-stale-while-revalidate keeps redirects fast during refresh.

deepDives.title: Eviction
points: LRU by default; size the cache to fit the measured hot set, not an arbitrary percentage of all keys.
Avoid 'allkeys-random' unless memory pressure is unavoidable.

bottlenecks.issue: Cache stampede on a popular key expiry.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Probabilistic early expiration or singleflight refresh per app server.

bottlenecks.issue: Cache cluster failure causes DB overload.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Multi-AZ replicas; circuit breaker that falls back to DB with strict rate limits.

bottlenecks.issue: Cache/DB staleness after writes.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Update or invalidate on write; short TTLs as a backstop.

talkingPoints: Distinguish read-through, write-through, write-behind, and cache-aside; we use cache-aside + write-through here.
Memory cost vs hit-rate is a knob — quantify it from capacity numbers, not vibes.

flows.name: Redirect — cache-aside read
flows.note: Hot codes are served from the cache; the DB is touched only on misses, and we backfill the cache on the way out.

messages.label: SELECT long_url WHERE code=? AND active

flows.name: Shorten — write-through populate
flows.note: Inserting into the cache on create means the first redirect is already a hit, avoiding a cold lookup.

steps.title: 5. Dedicated ID Generation Service
steps.description: Generating short codes by inserting and reading back an auto-increment ID couples write throughput to the database. Introduce a separate ID generation service (e.g. a counter service, Snowflake-style IDs, or a pre-allocated ID range) so that app servers can mint short codes without contending on a single DB sequence.
steps.decisionPrompt: Choose a short-code generation strategy that avoids collisions at high write volume without putting every create request through one bottleneck.

concepts.definition: A strategy that prevents two create requests from receiving the same short code.
concepts.whyItMatters: A collision would send one short URL to the wrong destination.

concepts.definition: Represent an integer using digits, lowercase letters, and uppercase letters.
concepts.whyItMatters: It creates compact, URL-safe codes from numeric IDs.

options.name: Range allocation (default)
options.description: Each app server claims a block of IDs from a central range allocator and then mints short codes locally from that block, encoding the monotonic integers to base62.
pros: App servers can mint IDs locally after claiming a block
Short ID-generator outages do not stop creates immediately
Produces compact monotonic integers that encode cleanly to base62
cons: Some IDs are wasted when an app server dies with an unused block
The range allocator is still a coordination dependency
Predictable codes may need scrambling if enumeration is a concern

view.caption: Each app server draws a local ID range from the range allocator, so it mints codes without per-request coordination.

options.name: Snowflake-style IDs
options.description: Each app server generates Snowflake-style IDs from a timestamp plus a machine id and sequence, so codes are minted independently with no central counter.
pros: No central counter on the hot path
Works naturally across many app servers and regions
IDs carry time and node information for debugging
cons: Clock rollback must stop ID issuance or use a fallback sequence
IDs are longer than a simple counter if encoded directly
Machine-id assignment becomes operationally important

view.caption: Each app server generates Snowflake-style IDs locally, coordinating only on a shared clock-sync source.

deepDives.title: ID strategies
points: Range allocation: each app server claims a block of IDs (e.g. 10,000) from a central counter. Encodes to base62. Coordination is cheap.
Snowflake-style: 41-bit timestamp + 10-bit machine id + 12-bit sequence. No central counter; clocks must be reasonably synced.
UUID + truncate: simplest, but collisions become non-trivial as cardinality grows.

deepDives.title: Custom aliases coexisting with generated codes
points: Use the same urls table; vanity aliases go through a UNIQUE check.
Reserve a separate namespace prefix to avoid collision with generated codes (e.g. all generated codes start with a lowercase letter).

bottlenecks.issue: ID generator itself becomes a SPOF.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Run replicas; for range allocation, app servers can survive short outages from their reserved block.

bottlenecks.issue: Clock skew on Snowflake-style nodes can produce duplicates.
bottlenecks.mitigation: NTP discipline; refuse to issue IDs when clock moves backwards.

talkingPoints: Choosing base62 over base64 — base62 is URL-safe without escaping.
Discuss code length vs collision probability vs guessability (security).

If monotonic IDs are base62-encoded directly, adjacent codes are guessable and crawlable — scramble (e.g. Feistel/multiply-by-coprime permutation) or add random bits so enumeration can't walk the namespace.

flows.name: Shorten — mint code via ID Generator
flows.note: The DB no longer owns ID generation; app servers ask the generator for the next code, decoupling write throughput from a single DB sequence.

flows.name: Reserve an ID range
flows.note: With range allocation the generator is not called for every create; app servers refill only when their local block is almost empty.

messages.label: take next local ID and base62 encode

view.caption: App servers call a dedicated ID generator to allocate each short code, then use the Redis cache and database as before.

steps.title: 6. Shard the Mapping Store
steps.description: At the target scale, the mapping store cannot be treated as one database. With roughly 150B URLs over 5 years, the raw mapping data alone is about 75 TB before indexes and replicas. Shard by short_code hash so redirects and creates distribute across many storage partitions.
steps.decisionPrompt: Pick the shard key and routing model for the mapping store: optimize for redirect lookup first, then explain the cost for analytics and admin queries.

concepts.definition: Split one logical table across multiple database partitions.
concepts.whyItMatters: The mapping store can grow beyond the capacity of one database node.

concepts.definition: The value used to choose which shard owns a record.
concepts.whyItMatters: For redirects, short_code is the natural key because every lookup starts with it.

options.name: Hash-sharded SQL (default)
options.description: The mapping is split across SQL shards by short_code hash, so a router sends each redirect or create to exactly one shard while preserving in-shard constraints and transactions.
pros: Preserves SQL constraints and transactional alias creation within a shard
Primary-key redirect lookups route to exactly one shard
Can start from the earlier SQL design and split gradually
cons: Operationally heavy: resharding, backups, schema changes, and failover are harder
Owner dashboards need a separate owner index or fanout query
Cross-region active/active writes need extra conflict planning

view.caption: The shard router hashes short_code to pick one of SQL shards 1, 2, or 3 for the mapping.

options.name: Managed partitioned KV
options.description: The mapping lives in a managed partitioned key-value store that handles sharding, replication, and elastic throughput, with a separate owner index for relational queries.
pros: Partitioning, replication, and elastic throughput are built into the storage service
Redirects are simple code-key lookups
Conditional writes can protect custom aliases
cons: Cost can be high at 100k+ reads/sec and large retention
Hot keys and hot partitions still need cache and edge offload
Relational owner queries require secondary indexes or separate stores

view.caption: App servers store mappings in a managed partitioned KV store and query a separate owner/metadata index.

deepDives.title: Shard key
points: Use hash(short_code) for even distribution because the redirect path always has the code.
Do not shard by user_id for the mapping table; celebrity users can become hot and redirects do not know owner_id before lookup.
Keep a separate owner index for management pages such as listing a user's links.

deepDives.title: Owner index write semantics
points: Write owner_links in the same create flow as the mapping; the redirect path never reads it, so a brief lag only affects the owner's own list.
Best-effort sync write plus an async repair job (reconcile from the mapping shards) keeps it from blocking creates while staying eventually complete.
Denormalize status/expiry into the owner index so management pages can filter without fanning out to every short_code shard.

deepDives.title: Resharding
points: Use virtual buckets so moving capacity means remapping buckets, not every individual key.
During migration, route reads to old and new locations until the bucket is fully copied.
Writes for a moving bucket must be serialized or dual-written with a clear cutover marker.

bottlenecks.issue: One hot short code can overload a cache shard even if DB shards are balanced.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Serve the hottest codes at the edge and replicate hot cache keys.

bottlenecks.issue: Shard-router mistakes can create lost writes or split-brain reads.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Version the routing table and roll it out through a controlled config service.

bottlenecks.issue: Owner dashboards need queries that do not match the redirect shard key.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Maintain an owner_id -> short_code index populated on create.

talkingPoints: A 7-character base62 namespace has about 3.5T possible codes, enough for the stated 150B mappings with room, but 8 characters buys a much longer runway.
Sharding is required by storage size and operational limits, not by redirect QPS alone, because cache and edge absorb many reads.

flows.name: Redirect through shard router
flows.note: Cache misses route to exactly one database shard by short_code hash.

flows.name: Create mapping on one shard
flows.note: The generated code determines the write shard; custom aliases use the same conditional insert path.

messages.label: INSERT IF NOT EXISTS code -> URL

view.caption: Cache misses go through a shard router that hashes the short code to route reads and writes across DB shards 1, 2, and 3.

steps.title: 7. CDN, Analytics, and Async Pipeline
steps.description: Push redirects to the edge using a CDN that can resolve hot codes without round-tripping to the origin. Capture every redirect as a click event and send it through a message queue (e.g. Kafka) to an analytics store, decoupling the hot read path from analytics ingestion and aggregation.
steps.decisionPrompt: Draw the synchronous redirect path and the asynchronous analytics path: what must complete before the 301 or 302 response, and what can be delayed or dropped?

traps.trap: Treating the click queue as exactly-once and summing every delivered event
traps.why: Edge logs and consumers are at-least-once, so retries and replays double-count clicks.
traps.instead: Stamp events with a stable event_id and dedup within a window, or make aggregate counter updates idempotent.

traps.trap: Retaining every raw click event forever
traps.why: At 100k events/sec the raw stream dominates storage and query cost within weeks.
traps.instead: Keep raw events for a short window, then roll up to per-code minute/hour/day counters and sample heavy dimensions.

traps.trap: Letting a degraded analytics pipeline block or fail redirects
traps.why: If the redirect path waits on the click queue, consumer, or rollup store, an analytics outage becomes a redirect outage — the opposite of the priority order.
traps.instead: Redirects must never block on analytics: fire-and-forget to a small in-process buffer, and on sustained backpressure drop or sampling-mark events (counting what was dropped) or write to a durable local outbox to drain later.

concepts.definition: Record click events through a queue or stream after the redirect response is served.
concepts.whyItMatters: Analytics should not add latency or reduce availability of the redirect path.

concepts.definition: Serve frequently requested redirect data from infrastructure close to users.
concepts.whyItMatters: It reduces origin load and improves latency for popular links.

options.name: 302 + edge logs (default)
options.description: Redirects are served as temporary 302s with CDN edge logging feeding the analytics queue, so hot codes are offloaded yet expirations and click counts stay enforceable.
pros: Keeps click analytics reasonably accurate even when hot redirects terminate at the edge
Avoids long-lived browser caching for links that may expire or be deleted
Still offloads origin for hot codes
cons: More repeat traffic than permanent browser-cached 301 redirects
Requires CDN/edge logging integration with the analytics pipeline
Analytics remain eventually consistent and can drop during queue outages

view.caption: The CDN answers redirects with a 302 and logs each click to the queue, which streams events into the analytics store.

options.name: 301 for permanent links
options.description: Redirects are served as permanent 301s so browsers and intermediaries cache them aggressively, minimizing repeat traffic at the cost of accurate counts and easy deletion.
pros: Lowest repeat traffic because browsers and intermediaries cache aggressively
Good for links that never expire and do not need exact click counts
Reduces cost for extremely hot links
cons: Click counts become approximate because many repeat clicks never reach edge or origin
Deletes and expiration are hard to enforce once clients cache permanently
Bad fit for vanity links that owners may edit

view.caption: The browser cache and CDN serve a 301 permanent redirect, with click logging downgraded to best-effort into the analytics store.

deepDives.title: Edge resolution
points: CDN can do the redirect itself for hot codes via edge functions (Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge).
Origin still owns invalidation: on update/delete/expiry, publish a purge event and keep edge TTL bounded.
Long-tail (cold) codes fall through to origin; cache them in the regional Redis cluster.

deepDives.title: Analytics pipeline
points: Edge functions and app servers emit click events to Kafka (or Kinesis/Pub-Sub). Hot path doesn't wait on the write.
Stream processor (Flink/Spark) aggregates per-code counters and writes to OLAP (ClickHouse, BigQuery, Redshift).
Per-code counter in Redis gives near-real-time totals; OLAP gives historical/dimensional queries.
Retention: keep raw events for a short window (e.g. 7-30 days), then keep only minute/hour/day rollups in click_counters for long-term dashboards.

Backpressure: if the queue is down or lagging, the redirect still returns; events buffer in a small in-process queue and the oldest analytics are dropped on sustained outage (analytics is best-effort).

deepDives.title: At-least-once and deduplication
points: Edge logs and queue consumers are at-least-once, so the same click can arrive more than once.
Stamp each event with a stable event_id (e.g. hash of edge request id) and dedup within a window, or make aggregate updates idempotent so replays don't double-count.
On consumer restart, replay from the last committed offset; idempotent counters keep the rollups correct.

deepDives.title: Caching contract
points: Use 302 as the default if links can expire, be deleted, or need click analytics.
Offer 301 only as an explicit permanent-link mode with approximate analytics.
Set edge TTL to the lesser of cache policy and remaining link lifetime.

deepDives.title: Operations and SLOs
points: Track redirect p99 latency, CDN hit ratio, cache hit ratio, and cache stampede rate as the primary redirect-health signals.
Alert on 404/blocked redirect rate, ID-allocation failures, shard hotness, replication lag, and queue lag (analytics backlog).
Watch CDN purge failures and abuse-scan backlog so takedowns and safety gates are not silently delayed.
Page on user-visible reliability signals (redirect availability/latency), not on raw resource metrics alone.

bottlenecks.issue: Queue backpressure stalls app servers.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Async send with a small in-process buffer; drop oldest analytics on sustained outage (analytics is best-effort).

bottlenecks.issue: Edge cache poisoning (wrong/expired URL cached).
bottlenecks.mitigation: Signed cache keys; purge-on-write; short TTLs at the edge for mutable items.

bottlenecks.issue: Hot-key concentration overloads one cache shard.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Per-shard request coalescing; promote to multiple replicas; serve from the LB layer via in-memory micro-cache.

talkingPoints: Make the analytics tradeoff explicit: edge and browser caching reduce cost but make click counts eventually consistent and sometimes approximate.
Discuss the consistency contract: a freshly-created link may take a few hundred ms to be visible at every edge, but origin reads should work immediately.

flows.name: Redirect — edge-resolved (hot path)
flows.note: Hot codes are served entirely from the CDN, never reaching the origin. The bulk of redirect traffic terminates here.

flows.name: Takedown — block an abusive link
flows.note: An admin (or automated scanner) flags a code via the internal /internal/admin/block endpoint; the origin marks it blocked, invalidates the regional cache, and enqueues a durable purge_job that retries the CDN purge until acknowledged so the safety gate takes effect everywhere.

flows.name: Redirect — edge miss, origin + click event
flows.note: On a CDN miss the request falls through to the origin, which resolves via cache/DB and emits a click event asynchronously. The hot read path never waits on the queue.

view.caption: A CDN serves hot redirects at the edge, logging clicks to a queue that feeds the analytics store, while misses fall back to the app tier.

steps.title: 8. Multi-Region Redirects and Replication
steps.description: Redirect traffic should stay available and low-latency even during a regional outage. Run the edge, app tier, cache, and mapping shards in multiple regions. Choose an explicit write model: active-passive is simpler; active-active gives lower create latency but needs conflict-free ID allocation and replication lag handling.
steps.decisionPrompt: Choose a multi-region read/write model for redirects: keep global reads fast while making link creation, replication lag, and regional failover explicit.

concepts.definition: The delay between a write in one region and visibility in another region.
concepts.whyItMatters: A newly created link may not resolve everywhere immediately unless the design handles lag.

concepts.definition: Multiple regions accept traffic, and often writes, at the same time.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves global latency and availability but makes conflict handling more explicit.

options.name: Active-passive writes
options.description: One primary region owns all mapping writes and replicates to standbys; other regions serve redirects from read-only replicas and forward creates to the primary, failing over only by promoting a standby.
pros: Simplest correctness model for creates and custom aliases
One primary region owns mapping writes, so no cross-region conflict resolution
Read replicas in other regions still improve redirect latency
cons: Create requests from far regions pay cross-region latency to reach the primary
Standby replicas serve reads only; writes pause until a controlled failover promotes one
Regional write outage in the primary can pause new link creation until promotion

view.caption: GeoDNS sends creates to the primary region's apps and DB; the standby region serves redirects from a read-only replica that trails via async replication and is promoted only on failover.

options.name: Active-active writes
options.description: Every region accepts creates locally: generated codes use region-scoped ID prefixes (stamped with home_region, code_source='generated') and replicate between regions, while custom aliases go through a strongly-consistent global reservation before commit. Early cross-region redirect misses fall back to the link's home_region during replication lag.
pros: Low create latency in every region for generated codes
Regional write outage affects only local capacity if traffic can fail over
Pairs well with region-scoped ID prefixes or Snowflake worker bits
cons: Custom aliases need a global reservation service or home-region routing
Replication lag means a brand-new link may miss in another region briefly (mitigated by home-region fallback)
Conflict handling, replay, and observability are much harder

view.caption: Both regions accept writes: generated codes use a region ID prefix and reconcile through bi-directional replication, while custom aliases are claimed once via the global reservation service before commit.

deepDives.title: Read path
points: GeoDNS or anycast routes users to the nearest healthy edge.
Hot links are served at the edge; regional cache and DB shards cover misses.
If a region is unhealthy, traffic shifts to another region with warm enough cache capacity.

deepDives.title: Write path
points: Generated codes can be made conflict-free with region-scoped ID ranges or Snowflake worker bits.
Custom aliases are harder: either route each alias to a home region or use a globally consistent uniqueness check.
A new link may not be visible in every region until replication catches up; origin can route misses to the write home region during that window.

bottlenecks.issue: Replication lag causes a freshly-created link to 404 in another region.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Return the home region in metadata and route early misses there; keep edge TTLs short during the first minutes.

bottlenecks.issue: Custom alias conflicts across regions.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Use home-region routing or a global strongly consistent alias reservation service.

bottlenecks.issue: Failover can overload the surviving region.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Keep spare capacity, shed low-priority analytics, and pre-warm caches for globally hot links.

talkingPoints: Do not say active-active casually; explain how IDs, aliases, replication lag, and deletes behave.
The system can promise immediate consistency in the write region and eventual global visibility.

flows.name: Create in home region
flows.note: Generated codes avoid conflicts by using region-scoped allocation; replication makes the mapping visible globally.

flows.name: Regional failover redirect
flows.note: Reads fail over through the edge/geo layer; cold misses may route to the replicated shard in the surviving region.

view.caption: GeoDNS routes the client to the nearest region's edge and apps, each with its own cache and DB shards, kept in sync by a replication stream.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description:
End-to-end URL shortener architecture: public create and redirect APIs, stateless app servers, cache-aside hot redirects, dedicated ID generation, sharded mapping storage, CDN-assisted redirects, asynchronous analytics, replication, and observability.

Recommended write model: active-active for generated codes, single-owner for custom aliases. Every region accepts creates and mints generated codes from region-scoped ID ranges (or Snowflake worker bits), so they can never collide and writes stay local and fast. Custom (vanity) aliases are the one globally-unique namespace, so they go through a strongly-consistent global reservation (or are routed to a home region). Mapping writes are committed synchronously to the owning region's shard and replicated asynchronously; global visibility is eventual. During replication lag, an early redirect miss in another region routes to the link's home region before returning 404. On regional failover, generated-code creates continue in surviving regions, custom-alias reservation pauses until the reservation service is reachable, and all redirects keep serving from edge/cache/replica. CDN purges on delete/expiry/takedown are retried and tracked until acknowledged.

deepDives.title: Regional ownership contracts the diagram relies on
points: Generated codes: each region mints from a region-scoped ID range (or Snowflake worker bits) and stamps home_region/code_source='generated' on the row, so two regions can never produce the same code without coordination.

Custom aliases: the Global Alias Reservation service is the single strongly-consistent namespace. A region reserves the alias there before committing the mapping (code_source='alias'); on failover, generated-code creates continue while alias reservation pauses until the service is reachable.

Early-miss fallback: a redirect that misses in a non-home region during replication lag routes to the link's home_region before returning 404, so a brand-new link resolves globally within the lag window.
Durable purge: deletes, expiries, and takedowns write a purge_job that retries the CDN/edge purge until every edge acknowledges, so a safety takedown is never silently dropped.
Owner index: owner_links is written best-effort in the create flow and reconciled by an async repair job; the redirect path never reads it, so its lag only affects an owner's own dashboard.

options.name: Recommended multi-region redirect design
options.description: Active-active generated-code writes with a globally-reserved custom-alias namespace. Edge, app, cache, ID generation, and short_code-sharded mapping run in every region; redirects serve from CDN and cache while clicks flow off-path through a queue to analytics. Generated codes use region-scoped ID space (no cross-region conflict); custom aliases use a global reservation or home-region routing; early misses during replication lag fall back to the link's home region.
pros: Redirect reads are fast through CDN and cache layers
Create path remains strongly owned by app + mapping store
Click analytics are off the critical redirect path
Sharding by short_code keeps lookups direct
cons: Requires cache invalidation and replication-lag handling
Owner/admin queries need a secondary index
Multi-region writes add conflict and ID-space planning

view.caption: The recommended design routes clients through GeoDNS and the CDN to app servers that mint region-scoped codes, reserve custom aliases through a global reservation service, shard the mapping store, log clicks to analytics, and replicate across regions.

options.name: Simpler single-region design
options.description: A single region runs the full edge-to-shard stack with CDN, cache, ID generation, and async analytics, trading global latency and resilience for a much simpler operational story.
pros: Much easier to operate for moderate scale
Clear consistency story for create and redirect
Can still add cache and analytics without multi-region complexity
cons: Higher global latency
Region outage affects writes and cache misses
Scaling ceiling is lower before sharding/replication are added

view.caption: The simpler single-region design fronts app servers with a CDN and cache over one primary mapping DB plus a read replica, logging clicks to analytics.

The client reaches the nearest edge via GeoDNS, the CDN serves cached redirects, and app servers mint region-scoped codes, reserve custom aliases globally, read the cache, route to mapping shards, log clicks to analytics, and replicate cross-region.
